name: As A / Expert Frame
trigger: Credentials borrow trust upfront
template: As a [CREDENTIAL], here's what I'd never [DO/BUY].
why_it_works: Leading with expertise primes the viewer to take the advice as authoritative.
examples: As a dermatologist, here's what I'd never put on my face.
As a chef, this is the only knife I use.
As a financial advisor, stop doing this with your savings.
As a personal trainer, skip these 3 exercises.
As someone who's read 10,000 ads, this hook always wins.

applied_examples.hook: As an esthetician, here's the one product I tell everyone to throw out.
As a CFO, here's the one report I check before any other.

name: As A Professional
trigger: Credentials grant instant trust in the claim
template: As a [PROFESSION] with [X] years, here's my take on [TOPIC]
why_it_works: Stated expertise makes the advice feel credible and worth heeding.
examples: As a UX designer with 8 years experience, here's why most pages don't convert.
As a nurse, here's what I'd never do.
Speaking as someone who's done this 1,000 times...
I've spent 10 years on this, so trust me.

applied_examples.hook: As an esthetician, here's the product I'd never buy.
As an accountant, here's the deduction everyone misses.

name: Borrowed Authority
trigger: expert deference, credibility transfer
template: A [credentialed expert] told me [surprising thing], and it changed everything.
why_it_works: People defer to credible experts, so borrowing authority front-loads trust into the claim.
examples: A Harvard sleep scientist told me to stop doing this.
A retired FBI negotiator taught me this one line.
A Michelin chef told me the salt rule is wrong.
A burnout therapist gave me this 10-second test.
A top VC told me what actually kills startups.

applied_examples.hook: A child psychologist told me to never say this phrase. Here's why.

name: Credential Lead
trigger: Stated expertise grants instant trust in the claim
template: As a [PROFESSION] With [X] Years Experience, Here's [INSIGHT]
why_it_works: Leading with credentials makes the viewer accept that the speaker actually knows the truth.
examples: As a Nurse With 12 Years on the Floor, Stop Doing This
As a Lawyer, Never Say This to Police
As a Pilot, This Is the Only Seat I Book
As a Chef, I'd Never Order This at a Restaurant
As a Recruiter, This Resume Line Gets You Hired
As a Mechanic, Here's What Dealers Overcharge For

applied_examples.hook: As an Agent of 15 Years, This Is the Question Buyers Never Ask

name: Here's What Actually Works
trigger: Cuts through noise with the proven truth
template: Here's what actually works (and what doesn't) with [TOPIC]
why_it_works: Positions the creator as the filter between hype and reality.
examples: Here's what actually works (and what doesn't).
The truth about what actually works with [topic].
Forget the hype, here's what really matters.
Here's what finally worked for me.

applied_examples.hook: Here's what actually works for belly fat (not what you think).
Here's what actually grows an account in 2026.

name: Honest Review After Time
trigger: Long-term testing signals trustworthy verdict
template: An honest review of [PRODUCT] after [TIME] of use.
why_it_works: A duration-based verdict reads as earned, unbiased experience rather than a paid plug.
examples: An honest review of this after 6 months of daily use.
I used this every day for a year — here's the truth.
Unbiased review of the viral [product] after 30 days.
What 90 days with this actually did.
My honest thoughts after wearing these for a month.

applied_examples.hook: Honest review of these 'walk all day' shoes after 60 days.
An honest review of this CRM after running my agency on it for a year.

name: I Analyzed N
trigger: Large sample size promises a data-backed pattern reveal
template: I Analyzed [BIG NUMBER] [THINGS] and Found [PATTERN]
why_it_works: A large analyzed sample implies a real, statistically credible insight you couldn't get alone.
examples: I Analyzed 1,000 Viral Videos and Found This
I Studied 500 Millionaires — They All Did This
I Read 200 Cold Emails That Got Replies
I Watched 100 TED Talks So You Don't Have To

examples: As an ER nurse for 12 years, here's what I'd never do.
As a recruiter, here's the resume line I always skip.

name: Numbered Mistakes You're Making
trigger: Combines list payoff with personal error callout
template: [NUMBER] mistakes [AUDIENCE] make with [TOPIC]
why_it_works: Finite list plus the worry that you're guilty of one of them.
examples: 5 mistakes you're probably making when you [task].
3 mistakes new freelancers make in their first 90 days.
3 mistakes you're making right now.
Everyone makes this mistake with [topic].

applied_examples.hook: 4 mistakes killing your Google reviews.
3 mistakes ruining your manicure.

name: Numbered Things I Wish I Knew
trigger: Curiosity plus a defined, finite payoff
template: [NUMBER] things I wish I knew before [MILESTONE]
why_it_works: Promises specific, countable lessons with regret-tinged value.
examples: 3 things I wish I knew before doing this.
5 things I wish I knew when I started.
Seven things you wish you knew before launching a course.
5 things nobody tells you about [topic].

applied_examples.hook: 5 things I wish I knew before buying my first home.
3 things I wish I knew before getting lash extensions.

name: Odd-Number List
trigger: Specific odd count promises finite, organized payoff
template: [ODD NUMBER] [THINGS] That [DESIRABLE OUTCOME]
why_it_works: Odd numbers feel more authentic and outperform even ones, while the count promises digestible value.
examples: 7 Habits That Doubled My Income
3 Signs You're About to Burn Out
5 Things I Wish I Knew Before 30
9 Mistakes Beginners Always Make
23 Reasons You're Still Broke
7 Tools That Replaced My Whole Team

applied_examples.hook: 5 Onboarding Mistakes Killing Your Free Trials

name: Oddly Specific Number
trigger: Concrete figures feel authentic and real, sparking why-that-number
template: [DOING THING] [EXACT LARGE NUMBER] Times
why_it_works: A precise, weird number signals a real story and makes vague claims feel tangible and credible.
examples: Going Through the Same Drive Thru 1,000 Times
I Said Yes for 50 Hours Straight
I Counted to 100,000
I Planted 20,000,000 Trees
I Opened 10,000 Mystery Boxes
Watching 1,000 Sunrises in a Row

applied_examples.hook: I Flipped 1,000 Pancakes to Find the Perfect Recipe

name: Only One Percent Know This
trigger: Exclusivity: rare knowledge few possess
template: Only [SMALL %] of people know this about [TOPIC]
why_it_works: Scarcity of the knowledge makes it feel valuable and elite.
examples: Only 1% of people know this.
This is the only thing you need to know about [topic].
99% of people get this wrong.
Most people will never figure this out.

applied_examples.hook: Only 1% of people use this tax trick.
Only 1% of iPhone users know this setting exists.

name: The Money Math
trigger: Concrete cost comparison makes value tangible
template: I spent [BIG AMOUNT] on [OLD WAY]. This costs [SMALL AMOUNT].
why_it_works: A vivid dollar comparison makes savings and value impossible to ignore.
examples: I spent $1,200 a year on this. This costs $30.
$8 a day vs $8 a month — same result.
Here's the actual cost breakdown nobody shows you.
I did the math and it's almost free.
What you're really paying for that subscription.

applied_examples.hook: I spent $400 a year on lattes. This setup cost me $40 once.
We paid 5 tools $900/mo. This one does it all for $99.

name: The Numbered List
trigger: Specific count signals scannable, finite value
template: [NUMBER] [THINGS] that [DELIVER OUTCOME].
why_it_works: A concrete number sets a clear, low-effort promise the brain wants to complete.
examples: 5 things I'd do differently starting over.
3 reasons this beats the expensive version.
7 habits that doubled my income.
4 mistakes killing your sleep.
3 apps that replaced my entire workflow.

applied_examples.hook: 3 reports we built that cut our reporting time in half.
5 products under $15 that changed my skin.

name: Things I Wish I Knew
trigger: Hindsight regret promises shortcuts to avoid the pain
template: [NUMBER] Things I Wish I Knew Before [MILESTONE]
why_it_works: Framing lessons as hard-won regrets makes them feel valuable and lets viewers skip the mistakes.
examples: 4 Things I Wish I Knew Before Hitting 100K Followers
5 Things I Wish I Knew Before Buying a House
3 Things I'd Tell My Younger Self
Things Nobody Tells You Before Starting a Business
What I Wish I Knew Before My First Job
If I Could Start Over, I'd Do These 5 Things

applied_examples.hook: 7 Things I Wish I Knew Before Launching My Course

name: Three Reasons Why
trigger: Stacked justification builds a persuasive case
template: 3 reasons [PRODUCT/IDEA] is better than [ALTERNATIVE].
why_it_works: A small, finite number of reasons feels digestible and builds momentum toward a conclusion.
examples: 3 reasons this beats the name brand.
3 reasons I'll never go back.
3 reasons your ads aren't converting.
3 reasons to ditch your morning coffee.
3 reasons this is worth every penny.

applied_examples.hook: 3 reasons this $25 version beats the $200 one.
3 reasons we left our old CRM (and never looked back).

name: Tier List Provocation
trigger: ranking debate plus controversy bait
template: Ranking [category] worst to best. #1 will surprise you / my F-tier will start a fight.
why_it_works: Rankings invite disagreement, and a teased controversial pick guarantees watch-through.
examples: Ranking fast food breakfasts worst to best. #1 shocked me.
Putting productivity apps in tier list. F-tier will anger people.
Ranking every diet I tried. The winner was free.
Tier-listing morning habits. Most are useless.
Ranking side hustles by real ROI. Top one surprised me.

applied_examples.hook: Ranking every note app worst to best. My F-tier pick will make people mad.

name: X Reasons Why
trigger: Highest-performing list frame promises explanatory closure
template: [NUMBER] Reasons Why [SURPRISING CLAIM]
why_it_works: 'X reasons why' is the top-performing list opener because it promises both a count and an explanation.
examples: 7 Reasons Why You're Always Tired
3 Reasons Why Your Content Isn't Growing
5 Reasons Why Smart People Stay Broke
10 Reasons Why You Should Quit [THING]
6 Reasons Why Nobody Replies to Your Emails
4 Reasons Why This Always Fails

applied_examples.hook: 5 Reasons Why Shoppers Abandon Your Cart

name: Awe Reveal
trigger: awe, scale or beauty arrests attention
template: You're looking at [astonishing thing], and what it does is unbelievable.
why_it_works: Awe captures attention and triggers sharing, holding the viewer in wonder.
examples: You're looking at the largest living thing on Earth.
This tiny chip holds more than every library combined.
What this machine does in one second took humans a year.
You're seeing a sound made visible.
This is what one drop of ocean water actually contains.

applied_examples.hook: You're looking at a photo of a single atom. What it reveals is staggering.

name: I Tried The Trend
trigger: Riding a trend plus uncertain result invites the verdict
template: I Tried [VIRAL TREND] for [TIME] — Here's What Happened
why_it_works: Combining a known trend with an unknown personal outcome leverages familiarity and curiosity at once.
examples: I Tried the 5AM Club for 30 Days
I Let AI Plan My Entire Week
I Tried Living on $5 a Day
I Did the Viral [TREND] So You Don't Have To
I Tried Every Productivity Hack on TikTok
I Followed [Famous Person]'s Routine for a Week

applied_examples.hook: I Tried the 75 Hard Challenge — Here's the Truth

name: Mid-Action Cold Open
trigger: Dropping into peak action breaks the scroll pattern
template: [Open mid-action / on a shocking visual, then] Let me explain
why_it_works: Starting at the most intense moment interrupts the scroll and forces a how-did-we-get-here question.
examples: Okay So This Just Happened...
Don't Ask How I Got Here
So This Is Apparently My Life Now
[On camera tearing up a resume] Here's Why
Three Seconds Before This Went Horribly Wrong
You're Probably Wondering How I Ended Up Like This

applied_examples.hook: So I'm Stuck at This Airport for 14 Hours — Watch This

name: Non-Sequitur Open
trigger: Surprise; an unexpected line demands explanation
template: [Odd or seemingly unrelated statement]. (You'll see why in a second.)
why_it_works: A line that seemingly has nothing to do with the offer creates a gap the reader must resolve.
examples: Why are the wheels crooked?
There's a reason I keep a brick on my desk.
This has nothing to do with money. At first.
My horse is laughing all the way to the bank.
I almost didn't write this.

name: Object Destruction Open
trigger: Shocking physical act demands an explanation
template: Open by [BREAKING/RIPPING/POURING] something unexpected
why_it_works: A startling action creates a why-did-they-do-that curiosity gap.
examples: [Rips up resume] Stop sending these.
[Pours out the protein] This is why you're bloated.
[Throws phone on couch] Do this first thing every morning.
[Smashes the jar] You're wasting half of this.

applied_examples.hook: [Cuts up credit card] Do this before you do anything else.
[Tosses the product in trash] Stop using this.

name: Pattern Interrupt Shock
trigger: novelty, breaks autopilot scrolling
template: [Unexpected action/statement that defies what they expected].
why_it_works: Something that violates expectations snaps the brain out of autopilot and onto your video.
examples: Stop. Don't make this dinner tonight.
Throw away your to-do list. Seriously.
I'm about to ruin your favorite snack for you.
Delete this app right now, I'll explain.
Put down your coffee for this one.

applied_examples.hook: Cancel that subscription before you finish this video.

name: Plot Twist
trigger: Sudden reversal resets attention mid-message
template: [SETUP that seems normal]... plot twist: [UNEXPECTED TURN].
why_it_works: An abrupt narrative reversal re-engages a viewer who thought they knew where it was going.
examples: I thought this was a scam... plot twist, it works.
Started as a joke. Ended up changing my routine.
Bought it ironically. Now I'm obsessed.
Expected to return it. Plot twist: I bought two more.
This was supposed to be a hate review.

applied_examples.hook: Bought this as a gag gift. Plot twist: I stole it for myself.
Signed up to cancel after the trial. Plot twist: I'm still here.

name: Slippery First Line
trigger: Frictionless momentum; nothing to resist
template: [2–4 word fragment].
why_it_works: A short, almost-incomplete first line is so easy to read the reader slides into the next sentence.
examples: It's easy.
Picture this.
Let me explain.
Here's the deal.
I goofed.
Read on.
sources: Joseph Sugarman, The Adweek Copywriting Handbook (thisiscopy.com)

name: Sudden Motion Interrupt
trigger: Unexpected movement hijacks visual attention
template: Open with abrupt [MOTION/GESTURE] toward camera
why_it_works: Sudden visual change reflexively stops the thumb mid-scroll.
examples: [Jumps into frame] Okay you NEED to see this.
[Snaps fingers] Hey, real quick.
[Lunges at camera] Wait, stop.
[Claps] Listen up.

applied_examples.hook: [Drops into a plank] Bet you can't hold this.
[Slams pan down] You're cooking eggs wrong.

name: The Skeptic Comment
trigger: Negativity overlay hijacks engagement instinct
template: [NEGATIVE COMMENT shown] — let me respond to that.
why_it_works: Reacting to criticism on-screen sparks engagement and feels like an honest rebuttal.
examples: Someone commented 'this is fake' — so let me prove it.
Reply to @user who said it won't work...
'You're just being paid to say this' — actually...
A hater said this is overpriced. Here's the math.
Got this comment and I have to address it.

applied_examples.hook: Comment said 'looks like every other serum.' Watch the side-by-side.
Someone said 'ads don't work anymore.' Let me show you last month.

name: The Unexpected Visual
trigger: Visual oddity breaks scroll pattern recognition
template: [STARTLING / OUT-OF-PLACE VISUAL] + [SPOKEN HOOK].
why_it_works: An image the brain can't immediately categorize forces a pause to make sense of it.
examples: [Product covered by a sticky note] Guess what's under here.
[Dropping product, reversed so it flies back to hand]
[Whispering to camera] Come closer, I'll tell you a secret.
[Holding up megaphone] Attention, this is important.
[Text appearing in sunglasses reflection]

applied_examples.hook: [Post-it over the product] What's hiding under this changed my routine.
[Phone glowing oddly] This notification saved me $300.

name: Two-Word Provocation
trigger: extreme brevity disrupts, demands completion
template: [One or two jarring words]. [Then the turn.]
why_it_works: Radical brevity feels like a record scratch, breaking the scroll rhythm and demanding the rest.
examples: Stop dieting.
You're overpaying.
It's a scam.
Quit now.
Read this twice.

applied_examples.hook: Delete it. Your best post is the one you're afraid to publish.

name: Wait, Stop, Don't
trigger: Verbal alarm halts the scroll reflexively
template: Wait. Stop. Don't [ACTION] before you see this
why_it_works: Urgent commands break the passive scroll trance instantly.
examples: Wait, stop, don't buy this until you watch this.
Stop what you're doing.
Hold on, you need to hear this.
No no no, don't do that.

applied_examples.hook: Stop, don't check out until you use this code.
Wait, don't book that hotel yet.

name: You've Never Seen This
trigger: Claimed novelty dares the viewer to verify uniqueness
template: You've Never Seen Anything Like [THING]
why_it_works: Asserting total novelty challenges viewers to confirm whether it's really as new as claimed.
examples: You've Never Seen a [THING] Like This Before
This Has Never Been Done Until Now
I Bet You've Never Seen This Trick
First Time Anyone's Tried This
This Shouldn't Be Possible, But...
Nobody's Ever Built One Like This

applied_examples.hook: You've Never Seen a Keyboard Do This Before

name: Are You Guilty
trigger: Self-reflection; an uncomfortable yes
template: Are You [embarrassed by / missing out on] [topic]?
why_it_works: A direct question pulls the reader into self-diagnosis, and the answer feels personal.
examples: Are You Ashamed of the Smells in Your Home?
Is the Life of a Child Worth $1 to You?
Can You Talk About Books With the Rest of Them?
Are you quietly overpaying for insurance?
Are you the last person still doing it the hard way?

name: Are You Tired Of
trigger: Names a pain point the viewer immediately recognizes
template: Are you tired of [PAIN POINT]? Try this
why_it_works: Calls out a frustration so accurately the viewer feels understood.
examples: Are you tired of [issue]? Try this!
Tired of [problem]? Here's the fix.
Struggling with [problem]? Try this.
Do you have problems with [thing]? I found the solution.

applied_examples.hook: Tired of lower back pain? Try this one stretch.
Tired of foundation creasing? Here's the fix.

name: Bet You Didn't Know
trigger: knowledge gap plus mild challenge
template: I bet you didn't know this about [familiar thing].
why_it_works: A friendly challenge to the viewer's knowledge baits them to prove or disprove it.
examples: I bet you didn't know your phone does this.
Bet you didn't know why we yawn.
I bet you've never noticed this on a dollar bill.
Bet you didn't know this about your own name.
I guarantee you didn't know this hides in plain sight.

applied_examples.hook: I bet you didn't know airlines do this with your seat assignment.

name: Diagnostic Question
trigger: Self-assessment loop; an unanswered question nags
template: When was the last time you [desirable action/feeling]?
why_it_works: A pointed question forces the reader to mentally answer, and the answer pulls them into the copy.
examples: When was the last time you opened your analytics and felt genuinely excited?
How much revenue are you leaving on the table every month?
What would change if your email list doubled in 90 days?
When did you last finish a workday actually finished?
How many hours a week do you lose to tasks a robot could do?

name: Did You Know
trigger: Direct knowledge query opens a gap the viewer wants closed
template: Did you know [SURPRISING FACT about everyday thing]?
why_it_works: A yes/no factual question surprises with knowledge most viewers don't have, compelling the reveal.
examples: Did you know that [surprising fact]?
I bet you didn't know this about [topic].
Have you ever wondered why...?
Is it just me, or [observation]?
Did You Know This Book Is Poisonous?
Did You Know Your Phone Tracks This?
Did You Know There's a Hidden Feature in [APP]?
Have You Heard About the World's Loneliest Toilet?
Did You Know You're Brushing Wrong?
Bet You Didn't Know This About [TOPIC]

applied_examples.hook: Did you know you've been storing eggs wrong?
Did you know your phone is tracking this?
Did You Know Your Router Has a Secret Speed Setting?
Did you know your pillowcase is dirtier than your toilet seat?
Did you know you can automate this in 2 taps?

name: Direct Question
trigger: open question demands internal answer
template: What would you do if [provocative scenario]?
why_it_works: A direct question hijacks attention because the brain can't help but start answering it.
examples: What would you do with one free day a week?
What if everything you know about saving is wrong?
Why does this keep happening to you?
What's the one thing you'd never admit out loud?
How much of your day do you actually control?

applied_examples.hook: What would you do if you got laid off tomorrow? Most people have no answer.

name: I Bet You Can't
trigger: Dare provokes the urge to prove you can
template: I bet you can't [DO X] without [REACTION]
why_it_works: A challenge triggers the competitive urge to watch and beat it.
examples: I bet you can't watch this without pausing to write one down.
Bet you can't do this.
Try not to laugh.
I challenge you to make it to the end.

applied_examples.hook: I bet you can't hold this for 30 seconds.
I bet you can't guess which one is the drugstore dupe.

name: The Provocative Question
trigger: Open question demands internal answer
template: What if [COMMON BELIEF] was actually [WRONG/OPPOSITE]?
why_it_works: A question forces the brain to engage and search for the answer in the content.
examples: What if your skincare is making it worse?
What if you didn't need 8 hours of sleep?
Why is no one talking about this?
What's the worst money advice you've gotten?
What if the algorithm isn't your problem?

applied_examples.hook: What if your bloating isn't about what you eat?
What if your churn problem is actually an onboarding problem?

name: What If
trigger: Curiosity plus hope; opens a desirable possibility
template: What if [hard thing] were actually [easy/possible]?
why_it_works: A hypothetical reframes the reader's assumed limits and motivates them to read for the proof.
examples: What if you never had to chase a late payment again?
What if learning a language took 15 minutes a day?
What if your slowest month became your best?
What if the problem isn't you — it's your tools?
What if you could write a week of content in one hour?

name: What Would You Do
trigger: Hypothetical dilemma forces personal mental simulation
template: What Would You Do If [HIGH-STAKES SCENARIO]?
why_it_works: A vivid what-if forces viewers to mentally answer, hooking them into hearing your answer too.
examples: What Would You Do If You Lost Your Top Client Tomorrow?
What Would You Do With an Extra $10K This Month?
What Would You Do If Nobody Judged You?
Could You Survive a Week Without [THING]?
What's the First Thing You'd Cut If Money Got Tight?
Would You Take This Deal?

applied_examples.hook: What Would You Do If Your Income Vanished Tomorrow?

name: What Would You Do If
trigger: Hypothetical forces viewer to self-insert
template: What would you do if [SCENARIO]?
why_it_works: Engages viewers by making them mentally answer for themselves.
examples: What would you do if you lost your top client tomorrow?
What would you do if [scenario]?
Can you guess what happens next?
If you could only keep one, which would it be?

applied_examples.hook: What would you do if you lost your income tomorrow?
What would you do if your kid said this to you?

name: Which Of These
trigger: Forced self-selection; personal relevance
template: Which of these [number] [problems/mistakes] is [costing] you [X]?
why_it_works: A multiple-choice frame compels the reader to pick their answer, resolvable only in the copy.
examples: Which of These 5 Money-Making Skills Are You Ignoring?
Which of these mistakes is quietly killing your open rates?
Which of these 7 hidden fees is draining your account?
Which of these common foods is sabotaging your sleep?
Which of these little-known tax breaks do you qualify for?

name: Why Does Nobody
trigger: Shared grievance question validates a felt frustration
template: Why Does Nobody [ADDRESS COMMON FRUSTRATION]?
why_it_works: Voicing an unspoken shared annoyance makes viewers feel understood and eager for the answer.
examples: Why Does Nobody Talk About How Exhausting [X] Is?
Why Does [Common Thing] Always Go Wrong?
Why Is No One Fixing This Obvious Problem?
Why Does Everyone Pretend [X] Is Fine?
Why Don't They Teach This in School?
Why Is This Still So Hard in 2026?

applied_examples.hook: Why Does Nobody Talk About How Painful Migrations Are?

name: Am I The Only One
trigger: social validation, hunt for shared experience
template: Am I the only one who [secretly relatable behavior]?
why_it_works: It invites the viewer to confirm a private habit is normal, which is irresistibly engaging.
examples: Am I the only one who rehearses arguments in the shower?
Am I the only one who opens the fridge with no plan?
Am I the only one who pretends to look busy when the boss walks by?
Am I the only one who has 47 browser tabs open right now?
Am I the only one who replies 'lol' while completely stone-faced?

applied_examples.hook: Am I the only one who makes a to-do list just to feel productive?

name: I Can't Stop
trigger: Compulsive enthusiasm signals genuine love
template: I can't stop [USING / THINKING ABOUT] this [THING].
why_it_works: Genuine, slightly obsessive enthusiasm is contagious and feels authentic.
examples: I can't stop using this and here's why.
I'm genuinely obsessed and can't shut up about it.
This lives on my desk now and I can't stop.
I've used this every day for a month straight.
I keep coming back to this one thing.

applied_examples.hook: I can't stop reaching for this — it's replaced everything else.
I literally open this app 20 times a day now.

name: Nostalgia Trigger
trigger: nostalgia, identity-linked autobiographical memory
template: Remember when [shared past experience]? Here's what we got wrong.
why_it_works: Nostalgia creates a warm, identity-linked bond and pulls the viewer into a shared memory.
examples: Remember when we burned CDs for road trips?
Remember waiting all week for one episode?
Remember when the whole class went silent for the overhead projector?
Remember saving up for one game?
Remember when phones just made calls?

applied_examples.hook: Remember dial-up internet? Here's what that wait actually taught us.

name: POV
trigger: Self-recognition; the reader sees their exact moment mirrored
template: POV: you [hyper-specific relatable situation]
why_it_works: Drops the reader inside a precise scenario, triggering 'that's literally me' and an urge to share.
examples: POV: you finally finished the project at 2am and exported the wrong file
POV: the client wants 'one small change'
POV: you open your laptop to do one thing and lose an hour
POV: it's Sunday night and the Monday dread just hit
POV: your code worked yesterday and you changed nothing
POV: you just discovered the productivity app that does it all.
POV: it's Sunday night and you haven't started.
POV: your boss just said 'quick call?'
POV: you finally found the one.
POV: You Finally Take Your Own Advice

applied_examples.hook: POV: it's leg day and you regret everything.
POV: a customer walks in 2 minutes before closing.
POV: You Said 'One More Episode' at Lunch

name: POV Relatable Scene
trigger: self-recognition, immersive perspective
template: POV: you're [relatable situation] and [the awkward/honest truth].
why_it_works: Casting the viewer as the protagonist makes the moment feel like their own lived experience.
examples: POV: you're the only adult in the room and have no idea what you're doing.
POV: it's 2am and you're googling your own symptoms.
POV: you said 'you too' when the waiter said enjoy your meal.
POV: you're a working mom and this is the real morning routine.
POV: you finally have a free weekend and forgot how to relax.

applied_examples.hook: POV: payday hit and it's already gone by Tuesday.

name: POV Scenario
trigger: Immersion places viewer inside the moment
template: POV: you [RELATABLE SITUATION].
why_it_works: Second-person framing drops the viewer directly into a moment they recognize.
examples: POV: you finally found a foundation that matches.
POV: it's 2pm and you've crashed again.
POV: your skincare routine actually works.
POV: you opened your laptop and inbox zero.
POV: you're the friend who always has snacks.

applied_examples.hook: POV: you found a protein bar that doesn't taste like chalk.
POV: your monthly report builds itself while you sleep.

name: Problem Callout
trigger: Self-recognition; people attend to their own pain
template: Still [struggling with PROBLEM] every [TIME PERIOD]?
why_it_works: Naming the reader's exact frustration makes them feel seen, so they keep reading.
examples: Still losing an hour every morning hunting for your keys?
Frustrated with a job that drains you by Tuesday?
Tired of pouring money into ads that go nowhere?
Still copy-pasting the same email ten times a day?
Worried about the safety of your family while you sleep?

name: Status Aspiration
trigger: status signaling, identity aspiration
template: The [small habit] that separates [aspirational group] from everyone else.
why_it_works: Tying a behavior to a status group lets viewers aspire to belong by adopting it.
examples: The morning habit that separates millionaires from the rest.
What confident people do that you don't.
The one thing top 1% earners never do.
What people who never get sick all have in common.
The habit that separates leaders from followers.

applied_examples.hook: The one email habit that separates high performers from everyone else.

name: Tell Me Without Telling Me
trigger: In-group recognition through shared experience
template: Tell me you're [identity/type] without telling me you're [that type].
why_it_works: It rewards insiders with instant recognition and dares them to see themselves in the answer.
examples: Tell me you work in marketing without telling me you work in marketing.
Tell me you're a teacher without telling me.
If you've ever worked in retail, you know this pain.
If your [thing] looks like this, same.
Tell me you're an oldest sibling without telling me.
Tell me you work in tech without telling me you work in tech.
Tell me you grew up in the 90s without telling me.
Tell me you're burnt out without saying you're burnt out.
Tell me you're new here without telling me you're new here.

applied_examples.hook: Tell me you're a runner without telling me you're a runner.
Tell me you work in restaurants without telling me.
Tell me you skipped leg day without telling me you skipped leg day.

name: The Reminder
trigger: Gentle nudge feels caring, not salesy
template: This is your reminder to [SIMPLE BENEFICIAL ACTION].
why_it_works: A soft, friendly reminder lowers defenses and feels like advice from a friend.
examples: This is your reminder to drink water.
Reminder: you don't have to do it all today.
Your sign to finally try that thing.
Gentle reminder to take your supplements.
Reminder that rest is productive too.

applied_examples.hook: This is your reminder to actually take your magnesium tonight.
Your reminder that you don't have to track everything manually.

name: Tired of Wasting Money
trigger: Shared frustration plus implied relief
template: Tired of wasting money on [THING]? Same.
why_it_works: It mirrors the viewer's exact frustration and signals a solution is coming.
examples: Tired of wasting money on supplements that do nothing? Same.
Sick of throwing cash at ads that flop? Same.
Tired of buying products that overpromise? Me too.
Done wasting money on gym memberships you never use?
Exhausted by skincare that breaks you out? Same here.

applied_examples.hook: Tired of wasting money on vitamins you forget to take? Same.
Tired of paying for 6 tools that do one thing each? Same.

name: Without Telling Me
trigger: In-group identity play; participatory humor
template: Tell me you're [a type] without telling me you're [that type]
why_it_works: Turns identity into a game, inviting the reader to supply their own punchline.
examples: Tell me you're a freelancer without telling me you're a freelancer
Tell me you've worked in retail without telling me you've worked in retail
Tell me your startup is pre-revenue without telling me it's pre-revenue
Tell me you're an only child without telling me you're an only child
Tell me you skipped breakfast without telling me you skipped breakfast

name: You Know That Feeling
trigger: Named shared experience builds instant rapport
template: You know that feeling when [SPECIFIC MOMENT]?
why_it_works: Articulating a feeling the viewer has felt but never said makes them feel seen.
examples: You know that feeling when your jeans finally fit?
Ever written and deleted the same text six times?
You know when you post something and get 12 views?
That feeling when your skin clears up overnight?
